coredata.dc:title: Long-wavelength excitation of carbon dots as the probe for real-time imaging of the living-cell cycle process
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Hao
Wang, Huibo
Guo, Jiaqing
Ye, Shuai
Shi, Weilong
Peng, Xiao
Song, Jun
Qu, Junle
coredata.dc:description: Cell-cycle targeting probes are crucial for observing and understanding how different drugs affect the cell stages. Here, we designed and prepared fluorescent F and N co-doped carbon dots (FNCDs) with a long-wavelength excitation, excellent optical stability, high fluorescence quantum yield (57 %), good water-solubility, low toxicity, and anti-photobleaching. The FNCDs have a positively charged surface, which enables easy absorption into DNA and RNA in vitro. For living cells, FNCDs can disperse the 4T1 cell via endocytosis pathways, and label both the nucleolus and chromosome. Due to long-wavelength excitation and excellent anti-photobleaching, we used the FNCDs as probes to directly monitor the cell division of living 4T1 cells, and we obtained the whole progress of cell division for the same cell. The experimental data showed the different chromosome morphologies of living cells in the various stages of cell division. Because of the low cost of raw material and excellent optical stability, FNCDs will hopefully replace dyes as nuclear and chromosome probes. This may open new ways to apply FNCDs in modern biology and cytology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Long-wavelength excitation
Nucleolus
Chromosome
Cell cycle process

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The absolute quantum yield of FNCDs test
The property and the fluorescent stability of FNCDs
Nucleolus-targeting and cellular uptake mechanism of FNCDs
Dynamic monitoring of nucleoli and chromosomes by FNCDs during the cell cycle process

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of FNCDs and their application.

(a) The TEM and HRTEM images of FNCDs. (b) The diameter distribution of FNCDs. (c) Excitation–emission color map of FNCDs. (d) The excitation spectrum (black curve), emission spectrum (red curve) and UV–Vis absorption (blue curve) spectra of FNCDs. (e) The fluorescence quantum yield of FNCDs. (f) The FTIR spectrum of FNCDs. (g) XPS full scan spectrum of FNCDs. (h) High-resolution C 1s XPS spectrum of FNCDs. (i) High-resolution F 1s XPS spectrum of FNCDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Normalized color plot of fluorescence decay in different (a) NaCl solutions of concentrations, (b) pH values, (c) temperatures. (d) The effects of different guest molecules (10 mM) on the fluorescence of FCNDs. (e) Viability of 4T1 cells under different concentrations of FNCDs after 24 h.

(a) The CLSM images of living 4T1 cell treated with FNCDs and Red dot 1: (I) bright field (II) FNCDs (Excitation wavelength: 488 nm) (III) Red dot 1 (200× in water; excitation wavelength: 640 nm) (IV) merge of images. (b) The CLSM images of DNase treated 4T1 cell with FNCDs and Red dot 1: (I) bright field (II) FNCDs (Excitation wavelength: 488 nm) (III) Red dot 1 (200× in water; excitation wavelength: 640 nm) (IV) merge of images. (c) The CLSM images of RNase treated 4T1 cell with FNCDs and Red dot 1: (I) bright field (II) FNCDs (Excitation wavelength: 488 nm) (III) Red dot 1 (200× in water; excitation wavelength: 640 nm;) (IV) merge of images. (d) The CLSM images of living 4T1 cell treated with FNCDs and ASF-tdTomato: (I) bright field (II) FNCDs (Excitation wavelength: 488 nm) (III) ASF-tdTomato (Excitation wavelength: 561 nm). (e) The CLSM images of living 4T1 cell treated with FNCDs and Fib-tdTomato: (I) bright field (II) FNCDs (Excitation wavelength: 488 nm) (III) Fib-tdTomato (Excitation wavelength: 561 nm). The concentration of FNCDs is 50 μg mL−1. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) The diameter distribution of FNCDs, RNA and RNA/FNCDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra of the FNCDs and RNA/FNCDs under excitation of 488 nm. (c) The fluorescence lifetime of FNCDs and RNA/FNCDs. (d) The diameter distribution of FNCDs, DNA and DNA/FNCDs. (e) Fluorescence spectra of the FNCDs and DNA/FNCDs under excitation of 488 nm. (f) The fluorescence lifetime of FNCDs and DNA/FNCDs.

(a) The CLSM images of 4T1 cells (control). (b) The CLSM images of FNCDs treated 4T1 cells under the 37 °C. (c) The CLSM images of FNCDs treated 4T1 cells under the 4 °C. (d) The CLSM images of NaN3 treated 4T1 cells under the 37 °C. (e) The CLSM images of Amiloride treated 4T1 cells under the 37 °C. (f) The CLSM images of CPZ treated 4T1 cells under the 37 °C. (g) The CLSM images of MβCD treated 4T1 cells under the 37 °C. (h) The CLSM images of Genistein treated 4T1 cells under the 37 °C. (i) The fluorescence intensities of 4T1 cells after various treatments.

(a) After adding FNCDs, the FLIM images of 4T1 cells under different times. (b) The FLIM images of FNCDs in the nutrient solution. (c) The FLIM images of FNCDs in the cytoplasm. (d) The FLIM images of FNCDs in the nucleus. (e) The average lifetime of FNCDs in the nutrient solution, cytoplasm and nucleus, respectively. (f) After adding FNCDs, the CLSM images of 4T1 cells under different time. Scale bar: 20 μm.

(a) The CLSM images of chromosome with FNCDs and Red dot 1 f during mitosis (Incubated condition: 30 min., 37 °C). (b) The CLSM image of RNase treated 4T1 cell with FNCDs during mitosis. (c) The CLSM image of DNase treated 4T1 cell with FNCDs during mitosis. (d) Different morphology of chromosomes of FNCDs treated living cell and fixed cell during the cell cycle process. (e) Dynamic monitoring of nucleoli and chromosomes by FNCDs during the cell cycle process (prophase, metaphase, anaphase, telophase and interphase). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Long-wavelength excitation of carbon dots as the probe for real-time imaging of the living-cell cycle process

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, 518060, PR China

College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Devices and Systems of Ministry of Education and Guangdong Province, College of Physics and Optoelectronic Engineering, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen 518060, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices, Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, Suzhou, 215123, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices
Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-based Functional Materials and Devices, Institute of Functional Nano and Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Soochow University, Suzhou 215123, P. R. China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu, 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212003, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cell-cycle targeting probes are crucial for observing and understanding how different drugs affect the cell stages. Here, we designed and prepared fluorescent F and N co-doped carbon dots (FNCDs) with a long-wavelength excitation, excellent optical stability, high fluorescence quantum yield (57 %), good water-solubility, low toxicity, and anti-photobleaching. The FNCDs have a positively charged surface, which enables easy absorption into DNA and RNA in vitro. For living cells, FNCDs can disperse the 4T1 cell via endocytosis pathways, and label both the nucleolus and chromosome. Due to long-wavelength excitation and excellent anti-photobleaching, we used the FNCDs as probes to directly monitor the cell division of living 4T1 cells, and we obtained the whole progress of cell division for the same cell. The experimental data showed the different chromosome morphologies of living cells in the various stages of cell division. Because of the low cost of raw material and excellent optical stability, FNCDs will hopefully replace dyes as nuclear and chromosome probes. This may open new ways to apply FNCDs in modern biology and cytology.

ce:section.ce:para: The cell cycle is the process of one cell giving rise to two cells; it plays an significant role in the biological growth process [1]. As the cell cycle moves through its two phases (interphase and the mitotic phase), the nucleolus takes on a variety of different forms. In the interphase, the nucleolus remains mainly spherical and is the main place of ribosome synthesis and RNA assembly [2]. During the mitotic phase, the structure of the nucleolus begins to transform and gradually disappear, while chromosomes appear. Chromosomes have different forms when the cell is at different periods of division (including prophase, metaphase, anaphase, and telophase). Therefore, the morphological changes of chromosomes can reflect the process of cell division. Furthermore, understanding each phase of the cell cycle is critical for determining the cell cycle regulatory mechanisms [3], and the abnormally dividing cell can signal numerous diseases such as cancer [4]. Thus, monitoring the cell cycle with probes can help us to directly observe the normal or abnormal stages when the different drugs treated the cells. For the human diseases, the cell cycle regulation plays an important role. So, we are necessary to develop a new kind of probe that can label the nucleolus and chromosome, and directly show the process of cell division. More vitally, due to the nucleolus and chromosome mainly containing DNA and RNA, the new type of fluorescent probe needs to bind to DNA and RNA.

Recently, scientists have developed many methods of cell cycle detection [5–9]. However, in order to monitor the mitotic progression, current protocols require the observed cells either to be fixed and permeabilized before the analysis [8,9] or to be fluorescently labeled by genetic engineering [5–8]. Organic dyes have also been widely used as fluorescent probes to observe the cell division process [5,10]. In 2018, Zong-Wan Mao et al. were the first to directly observe the dynamic process of living-cell division by fluorescence imaging using cyanine molecules [11], even though most people had been known the phenomenon for decades [12–14]. However, since organic dyes get photobleached easily, and have specific excitation wavelengths, low stokes-shifts and lifetimes, they have been limited in the cell imaging field. Thus, monitoring the entire cell-cycle progression in living cells, that has been a challenge. With the rapid development of nanoscience, a variety of nanomaterials have been prepared for various applications [15]. The optical properties of nanomaterials are superior to those of organic dyes used for cell imaging. As a result, nanomaterial use has appeared in nucleolus imaging, including noble metal nanocluster and quantum dots (QDs) [16–20]. Due to its ultrasmall size, good biocompatibility, and extraordinary stability, the noble metal nanocluster has been applied to living cell imaging [20]. However, their raw material is expensive and limits their practical application. For the inorganic QDs, although they have a high fluorescence quantum yield (QY), optical stability and stokes-shift, their low biocompatibility and high cytotoxicity limit their application in the field of living cell imaging. Furthermore, the targeting QDs need to modify the antibody protein, and the preparation method is complex [16–19]. Therefore, we need to develop a kind of new nanomaterial with a high QY and fluorescence stability, low toxicity, high stokes-shift and lifetime, wide excitation spectrum, and low raw material that does not require modification. More significantly, the new nanomaterial must be able to label the nucleolus and chromosome of the living cell.

Carbon dots (CDs) possess high water solubility, little to no toxicity, appreciable biocompatibility, excellent fluorescence stability, high stokes-shift and lifetime, and cheap raw materials and so on [21–24]. In recent years, the CDs as fluorescent probe are applied in the cell imaging field, such as the nucleus imaging [25], the nucleic acid structures imaging [26], lysosome imaging [27] and mitochondria imaging [28]. Thus, they are the best candidate material for imaging the living cell cycle, although their use to directly monitor the dynamic process of cell division has not been reported so far. However, traditional CDs also have some disadvantages, such as short-wavelength excitation and low QY in the aqueous solution. That is not conducive to long-time imaging of living cells and creates the need to develop a new type of CDs that possess targeting nucleoli and chromosomes, low toxicity, long-wavelength excitation and high QY in the aqueous solution.

So far, the foundation for cell cycle detection rests upon positively charged dyes that can be enriched in the nucleus by electrostatic interaction [5,29,30]. Thus, the surface of CDs should be positively charged. Furthermore, we think that the doping nitrogen (N), sulfur (S), or phosphorus (P) can effectively improve and change the properties of CDs [31,32]. In particular, doped N can generate the defects sites, and finally improve the fluorescence properties of CDs [24,33,34]. Additionally, doped F can alter the optical properties of graphene such as the transparency, photoluminescence and absorption band. Furthermore, elemental Fluorine (F) has the largest electronegativity in all the elements, that lead to easily form the hydrogen bond and increase luminescence stability [35–38]. Thus, co-doping CDs with N and F may further improve their properties such as surface charge, QY, and excitation and emission wavelengths.

In this work, we successfully prepare F and N co-doped carbon dots (FNCDs) using a simple hydrothermal treatment (Scheme 1 ). The QY of the prepared FNCDs is up to 57 % in the aqueous solution. For the different pH values, temperatures, ionic strengths, and guest molecules, FNCDs display excellent fluorescence stability. More crucial, the optical excitation wavelength is 466 nm, which is good for long-duration imaging of living cells with no damage. Due to their high QY and long-wavelength excitation, FNCDs make suitable fluorescent probes for living cell imaging. Furthermore, the surfaces of FNCDs possess a positive charge, allowing them to combine with negatively charged biological molecules (such as DNA and RNA). The experimental results indicate that FNCDs can enter living cells via endocytosis and absorb on the nucleoli by electrostatic interaction. Furthermore, due to their low toxicity, good optical stability, nucleolus and chromosome targeting, and long-wavelength excitation, FNCDs can monitor the living cell cycle in real-time and can completely display the whole cell division process.

The experimental materials were purchased from Adamas-beta, Beijing Chemical Reagent and Sigma-Aldrich. Before the experiment and tests, the all of solid samples and materials were dried under vacuum at 60 °C. All of experimental water is ultrapure water, their resistivity is 18 MΩ·cm−1.

The grain diameter distribution and Zeta potential of the FNCDs were collected with Zeta sizer Nano ZS, ZEN 3690, Malvern. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of FNCDs data were carried out on a FEI/Philips Tecnai G2F20 TWIN TEM. Bruker Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Hyperion) and KRATOS Axis ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer measures the fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of FNCDs. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy and fluorescence properties data were obtained using a GBC UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Cintra 2020) and Horiba Jobin Yvon luminescence spectrometer (Fluoro Max-4). We use the Nikon (A1R MP+) and Carl Zeiss (LSM 800 with Airyscan) to record the cell imaging data. The fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) images of FNCD-treated living cells were recorded on the FLIM image system (t ime-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system with a Becker &Hickl SPC120 unit and Nikon Eclipse confocal microscope system).

16.7 mmol glycine, 6.3 mmol 2, 4-difluorobenzoic acid, 2 mL ethanol and 25 mL ultrapure water were added into Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. And it was heated and maintained at 160 °C for 4 h. The reaction solution removed into the dialysis bag (500 Da) when the temperature decreased to 25 °C. The solution of FNCDs was put into the lyophilize and freeze-dried (−60 °C and 10 Pa). Finally, the orange powder of FNCDs was obtained and redissolve in water for quantification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The absolute quantum yield of FNCDs test
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 5 mg FNCDs dissolved in 10 mL H2O and prepare 0.5 mg mL−1 FNCDs solution. Secondly, 300 μL FNCDs solution (0.5 mg mL−1) was added in 3 mL H2O and 50 μg mL−1 FNCDs solution was obtained. Finally, 3 mL FNCDs solution (50 μg mL−1) was added in the quartz colorimetric dish. This dish put into the Hitachi 7000 fluorescence spectrometer and recorded the absolute quantum yield of FNCDs.

The 4T1 cells were cultured in a 96-well plate and put them into the cell incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. Different concentrations of the FNCDs were put into the 96-well plate. After 24 h, the viabilities of the cells were measured by MTT assay. The cells were lysed with acidulated sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS). We used the Bio-Rad 680, U.S.A. to record the value of absorbance at 570 nm.

First, the RNase (100 U) and DNase (100 μg mL−1) were added into two sets of prefixed 4T1 cell culture plates for 30 min, respectively. The fixed cells were then labeled with 10 μL of FNCDs (5 mg mL−1) for 20 min. After a PBS rinse, the cell imaging data were recorded by using the confocal laser scanning microscope (Excitation wavelength: 488 nm).

The 4T1 cell line was used as a model to carry out FNCD imaging. The concentration of FNCDs is 5 mg mL−1. Firstly, the 4T1 cells were cultivated in the incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. After that, the 20 μL FNCDs were placed into the 4T1 cells (culture medium: 2 mL). After 30 min, the cell culture plate was washed with a phosphate buffer saline solution three times; And we use the confocal fluorescence microscope to observe the cells (Excitation wavelength: 488 nm).

For FNCDs and Red Dot 1 of cell imaging, the 4T1 cells in cell medium were seeded on the petri dish. The concentration of FNCDs is 5 mg mL−1, 20 μL FNCDs was put into the culture dish (culture medium: 2 mL). After 30 min, the FNCDs treated 4T1 cells washing three times with phosphate buffer saline solution. And then the 4T1 cells and Red Dot 1 were co- incubated at 37 °C for 30 min again. Finally, we used the phosphate buffer saline solution to wash the 4T1 cells three times. We use the laser-scanning confocal fluorescence microscope to observe the 4T1 cells. (Excitation wavelength of FNCDs: 488 nm; Excitation wavelength of Red Dot 1: 640 nm).

For FNCDs, ASF/SF2-tdTomato (ASF-tdTomato) and Fibrillarin-tdTomato (Fib-tdTomato) staining, 4T1 cells were seeded into confocal dishes. After 24 h, the number of cell reached around 70 % confluence and were then transfected with either ASF/SF2-tdTomato or Fibrillarin-tdTomato plasmids by using Lipofectamine 3000 (Invitrogen) according to the instructions. Then the cells were subcultured for 24−48 hours before observation. We use the laser-scanning confocal fluorescence microscope to observe the 4T1 cells. (Excitation wavelength of ASF-tdTomato and Fib-tdTomato: 561 nm).

In the first day, 4T1 cells were seeded into confocal dishes and cultured overnight. The next day, 20 μL of FNCDs was put into the confocal dishes, and FLIM image recording began at once. FLIM images were continuously collected every 5 min., until 40 min. Then, we use SPC Image (Becker &Hickl) software to analyse the fluorescence lifetime data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The property and the fluorescent stability of FNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The highly fluorescent and water-dispersible FNCDs were prepared using glycine and 2, 4-difluorobenzoic acid as raw materials in the hydrothermal method. The size, morphology, structure, optical property and elemental composition of the produced FNCDs was analyzed using a variety of techniques. Fig. 1 a shown the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) of FNCDs, the experiment data indicated that the FNCDs dispersed well and had a diameter of about ∼5 nm. Furthermore, their lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, which matched the 100 facets of graphene [22]. The grain diameter distribution of FNCDs matched the result of TEM (Fig. 1b). The fluorescent properties of FNCDs were systematically studied their fluorescence and ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectrums. As shown in Fig. 1c, the excitation–emission color map, that indicate the center of emission of FNCDs solution was at 502 nm in the 3D fluorescence plots of the excitation-emission map. And as shown in Fig. S1, the as-prepared FNCDs exhibit the excitation-independent fluorescence emission property, the result indicates FNCDs contain few defects and displayed a band gap emission. Fig. 1d displayed the UV–vis spectrum of FNCDs, it includes three peaks; the peak at 235 nm corresponded to π−π* transition, the second peak at 284 nm attributed to n−π* transition. And, the third peak at 464 nm was a prominent excitonic absorption band. This experimental data indicated FNCDs possess the high conjugation and quality [39–41]. Furthermore, the maximum photoluminescent emission and optimal excitation wavelength of FNCDs are 502 nm (red line) and 466 nm (black line), respectively. The QY of FNCDs (50 μg mL−1) is 57 % in the aqueous solution (Fig. 1e). Based on the QY and excitation source of FNCDs, they are more suitable to be as a probe for living cells imaging.

Next, to determine the structure, elemental composition, and surface state of FNCDs, we measure their Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). As shown in Fig. 1f, the FTIR of FNCDs, the peaks at 3448∼ 3288 cm−1 are corresponded to OH and NH2 stretching vibration, respectively. The absorption peak at 1626 cm-1 indicates CO stretching of the amide bond, and peaks observed at 1412, 1216, and 1112 cm-1 originate from the stretching vibration of bending modes of CN, covalent CF bonding, and semi-ionic CF bonding, respectively [42,43]. Furthermore, XPS spectra were recorded to determine the elemental composition of FNCDs. As shown in Fig. 1g, FNCDs contains the C (62.5 %), N (14.1 %), O (18.9 %), and F (4.5 %) elements. Fig. 1h shows that there are four fitting peaks in the high-resolution C 1s spectrum: CC/CC (284.8 eV), CO/CN (286.1 eV), CO (286.8 eV), and CF (288.3 eV) [44–46]. Fig. 1i displays the high-resolution F 1s spectrum, the fitting peak at 686.5 eV corresponded to the semi-ionic CF, and the other fitting peak at 687.3 eV is assigned to the covalent CF [47–49]. As shown in Fig. S2, there are two fitting peaks in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum, they at 400.1 eV and 401.5 eV are assigned to NC and NH, respectively [50,51]. In the high-resolution O 1s spectrum of FNCDs (Fig. S3), there are two fitting peaks at 531.5 eV and 533.1 eV can be assigned to CO and CO, respectively. The experimental data indicate that FNCDs contain a variety of hydrophilic groups such as −OH, −COOH, –NH2, and C–F. Thus, FNCDs are highly water-soluble, which could allow for further bioconjugation. As shown in Fig. S4, the ζ potential of the FNCDs was +6 mV, it indicates the surface of FNCDs have positively charged. That is possibly due to the presence of F and N elements. So, they could combine with the negative nucleic acids via electrostatic energy.

Subsequently, the fluorescent stability of the FNCDs was studied under different conditions. Firstly, we studied the fluorescent intensity of FNCDs under its different concentrations. The experiment data indicate that FNCDs have the strongest fluorescent intensity when its concentrations is 50 μg mL−1. Next step, the fluorescent stability of the FNCDs was studied under 50 μg mL−1 (Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. 2 a and Fig. S6a, the fluorescent intensity of FNCDs shows the fluorescent intensity of FNCDs decreased with the increasing concentrations of NaCl (0–1 M NaCl). When the concentrations of NaCl is 1 M, the fluorescent intensity decreased of FNCDs decreased by 8%. Fig. 2b and Fig. S6b shows that the fluorescence intensity of FNCDs does not depend on the pH, and that there is also no significant change between pH = 3 and pH = 12. But the fluorescent intensity of FNCDs begins to increase from pH = 1 to pH = 2. The possible reason that the doped F element can absorb abundant H+ when the pH = 1–2. That decreased the fluorescence resonance energy transfer among the FNCDs and cause the fluorescence enhancement. Fig. 2c displayed the influence of different temperatures on the fluorescence intensity of FNCDs. The experimental data indicated the fluorescence intensity of FNCDs decreased as the temperature increased from 25 to 100 °C; the fluorescence intensity of FNCDs only reduced by 16 % when the temperature was 100 °C. More importantly, the variety of guest molecules (such as anions and cations) hardly affected the fluorescence intensity of FNCDs (Fig. 2d). Next step, we studied the cytotoxicity of FNCDs in the 4T1 cells. As shown in Fig. 2e, when the concentration of FNCDs was as high as 400 μg mL−1, the cell viability was up to 83 %. The experimental data indicated that the FNCDs had no cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility when its concentration was not higher than 100 μg mL−1. All of the above results, the FNCDs exhibit excellent fluorescence stability properties and biocompatibility. It could be regarded as good candidates for a living-cell imaging sensor, and its optimal conditions for the living-cell imaging were 37 °C, pH = 6.0∼8.0 and 50 μg mL−1 (the concentration of FNCDs).

Due to the fluorescence stability and high QY of FNCDs in the aqueous solution, we used the FNCDs to co-culture the 4T1 cells and explored the localization of FNCDs in the living cell. We found that the FNCDs can reach the nucleolus. To confirm that, we used the FNCDs and Red Dot 1 to labeled 4T1 cells (Fig. 3 a). As shown in Fig. 3a (II), the nucleolus emits strong fluorescence signals when 4T1 cells were irradiated 488 nm laser. Because the Red Dot 1 can specifically mark the nucleic acids, the nucleoli of 4T1 cell emit red fluorescence when they were irradiated 640 nm laser (Fig. 3a (III)). The merged images confirmed the FNCDs can stain the nucleolus (Fig. 3a (IV)). Because the nucleolus mainly contains most RNAs (especially the rRNA) and some DNAs, this result indicated that the FNCDs can adsorb on the RNA and DNA. In addition, we used the ribonuclease (RNase) and deoxyribonuclease (DNase) to explore the nucleolus-labeling mechanism of FNCDs. For the DNase-treated cells, FNCDs were still located in the nucleoli (Fig. 3b). In contrast, Fig. 3c shown the fluorescence signals of FNCDs and Red Dot 1 disappeared in the region of the nucleus (RNase treated the 4T1 cell). Two kinds of fluorescent proteins (ASF-tdTomato and Fib-tdTomato,) were used to further prove the FNCDs can target the nucleolus [52]. As shown in Fig. 3d, the ASF-tdTomato fluorescent protein can specifically label the nucleus (Fig. 3d (III)). The FNCDs-treated 4T1 cells also showed bright green fluorescent spots in the cells (Fig. 3d (II)). The merged figure implied that the bright green fluorescent spot was the middle of the red area, which was the nucleolus. Next, the Fib-tdTomato fluorescent protein was used to further ensure that FNCDs can strain the nucleolus. Fig. 3e indicate that the fluorescence signal of FNCDs and Fib-tdTomato can overlap well. Moreover, the FNCDs exhibited excellent fluorescent stability after being irradiated under 488 nm laser for 50 min (Fig. S7). The above results indicated that the FNCDs as a fluorescent probe can combine with the RNA and DNA, and selectively stain the nucleolus of living cells.

To further explore the nucleolus-targeting mechanism of FNCDs, we study the dynamic light scattering (DLS), photoluminescent spectrum (PL) and fluorescence lifetime of FNCDs, FNCDs/RNA and FNCDs/DNA. Due to the positive potential of FNCDs (ξ +6 mV), the FNCDs can bind with the negative potential of RNA (ξ -33.4 mV) and DNA (ξ -35.3 mV) by electrostatic adsorption (Fig. 4 and Fig. S4). Besides, the FNCDs contains the F element which possesses the largest electronegativity and can form a hydrogen bond with the hydrogen donor molecule. Based above two reasons, the FNCDs easily absorbed the DNA and RNA. To prove this point, the FNCDs were added into the RNA and DNA solution and formed FNCDs/RNA and FNCDs/DNA, respectively. For RNA, Fig. 4a shown the diameter of FNCDs/RNA slightly increased than the free FNCDs (from 61.8 to 110.5 nm), and the fluorescence intensity of the FNCDs/RNA had an obvious increase (7%) than that of FNCDs (Fig. 4b). Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime research demonstrates that RNA can enhance the fluorescence lifetime of FNCDs (the lifetime of FNCDs increased from 2.50 to 2.95 ns after the addition of RNA) (Fig. 4c). There are similar results for DNA, as shown in Figs. 4d–f, after the addition of FNCDs, the diameter of DNA slightly increased from 16.3 to 25.4 nm, and the DNA also enhanced the fluorescence intensity of FNCDs by about 6% without changing their PL peak position. Likewise, the lifetime of FNCDs can also be increased by DNA (the lifetime of FNCDs increased from 2.50 to 2.76 ns after the addition of DNA) (Fig. 4f). Based on the above experimental results, Fig. 4a and d implied FNCDs can adsorbed on the RNA and DNA, and form FNCDs/RNA and FNCDs/DNA compound. That is because of FNCDs possessing a positive charge (ξ +6 mV), which can allow them to be incorporated into the negatively charged nucleic acid (Fig. S4). Besides, the emission peak of FNCDs (black line), the FNCDs/RNA (red line) and FNCDs/DNA (red line) remained unchanged, that suggested the FNCDs were bonded to the RNA and DNA by non-covalent bonds. Besides, the FNCDs have basic group (-NH2) which easily combine with the nucleic acid (RNA and DNA). So, FNCDs can absorbed on RNA and DNA. For enhanced fluorescence intensity and lifetime, there are similar results have previously been reported and the reason may be the structural diversity of nucleic acids determining the specific interaction between FNCDs and nucleic acids [53,54]. And FNCDs contain abundant fluorine element that can early form a hydrogen bond with the DNA and RNA, thereby reducing the non-radiative processes associated with the molecular internal [11,53]. Besides, the viscous characteristic of the condensed nucleic acid can also affect the fluorescence intensity of FNCDs in the cell [11]. Therefore, we tested the effect of glycerol on the fluorescence of the FNCDs (20 μg mL−1). The results showed that the fluorescence of FNCDs enhances gradually with increasing solvent viscosity (Fig. S8); the fluorescence intensity of FNCDs (20 μg mL−1) increased over 200-fold in 80 % glycerol and its QY is about 27 % (Fig. S9). But because of the low viscous characteristic of the condensed nucleic acid, the fluorescence intensity of FNCDs was not significant in nucleus medium for the living cell (Fig. 3a). Based on the above data, the prepared FNCDs could be used as an efficient fluorescence probe to image the nucleoli of live cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nucleolus-targeting and cellular uptake mechanism of FNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: To confirm the mechanism of FNCDs entering the living cell, the experiments were performed to identify their cellular uptake pathways. Four types of endocytosis can effectively control nanomaterials enter into the cells, they mainly include caveolae-mediated endocytosis (CvME), micropinocytosis, clathrin-mediated endocytosis (CME), and lipid raft mediated endocytosis (LrME). Different temperatures and inhibitors NaN3, 5- (N, Ndimethyl)- amiloride hydrochloride (amiloride), chlorpromazine (CPZ), methyl-β-cyclodextrin(MβCD)) were used to identify the pathway of FNCD dispersion into the cell (Fig. 5 a–h). The results showed that a temperature of 4 °C and the inhibitor NaN3 can greatly affect the FNCDs entry into the cell, which indicated that the cell absorption of FNCDs was related to energy. Because different fluorescence intensities can reflect different cellular uptake pathways, we used a flow cytometer to quantify the fluorescence intensities. Compared with the FNCDs group (37 °C), we found that the fluorescence intensities of FNCDs had slightly declined (by 21, 29, 22 and 33 %, respectively) in four kinds inhibitor-treated cells. The data suggested that the endocytosis of FNCDs contains many kinds of pathways, such as temperature-/energy-dependent transport, macropinocytosis, LrME, CvME, and CME.

We used fluorescence lifetime imaging (FLIM) to show the DNA and RNA increasing the fluorescence intensity of FNCDs and to show the process of FNCDs entering the cell and reaching the nucleolus. Fig. 6 a shows the time-dependent experiment of FNCDs. The FNCDs had accessed the cytoplasm by endocytosis after 10 min. of co-incubation with 4T1 cells. After 20 min, FNCDs can arrive at the nucleolus. The different areas of FNCD-treated 4T1 cells show a color gradient that reflects the incubation time. The data implied that the lifetime of FNCDs begins to transform after they enter the cells. As shown in Fig. 6b–e, the average lifetime of FNCDs in the nutrient solution, cytoplasm, and nucleus were 2545.35, 3334.04, and 3702.49 ps, respectively. The time-dependent CLSM imaging experiment also showed that FNCDs can reach the nucleolus after 20 min. and that the optimal incubation time is about 30 min. More importantly, the result is consistent with the FLIM data (Fig. 6f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic monitoring of nucleoli and chromosomes by FNCDs during the cell cycle process
ce:section.ce:para: The nucleolus has different morphological structures when the cell is in different cell division cycles. It contains the most RNAs (especially the rRNA) and some DNAs when the cell is in the interphase of cell division. Conversely, the mitotic phase is marked by a large number of DNA. FNCDs can bond with DNA and RNA; thus, FNCDs can also stain the chromosome. To further prove the point, we co-stained cells with FNCDs and Red Dot 1 to confirm that the bright filamentous is the chromosomes. Fig. 7 a shown that the green fluorescence of FNCDs overlaps well with the red fluorescence of Red Dot 1 from the chromosomes. This result shows that FNCDs can stain the chromosomes during the mitotic phase. Next, we used RNase and DNase to prove FNCDs can combine with the DNA. As shown in Fig. 7b and 7c, the FNCDs still labeled the chromosome after the RNase digested the cells, and the morphology of the chromosomes was unchanged. By contrast, the morphology of chromosomes disappears in the DNase-treated cell (Fig. 7c). Thus, FNCDs can effectively locate chromosomes in the mitotic phase. In addition, FNCDs displayed different stages of cell division after incubation with living cells or fixed cells (Fig. 7d). As shown in Fig. 7e, in the beginning, there were three green spots in the nucleus; they disappeared as time elapsed, and the chromosomes began to appear in the cell. After that, the chromosomes lined up along the metaphase plate. Eventually, the chromosomes broke at the centromeres, and the sister chromatids moved to the opposite ends of the cell. At last, the nuclear membrane reformed, the nucleoli reappeared, and the chromosomes unwound into chromatin. There results indicate that FNCDs can monitor the cell-cycle in real-time.

In summary, FNCDs with high QY, excellent optical stability and long-wavelength excitation were successfully prepared via the one-step hydrothermal treatment of 2, 4-difluorobenzoic acid and glycine. Under different ionic strengths, pH values, temperatures and guest molecules, the prepared FNCDs showed excellent fluorescence stability. Additionally, the optical excitation wavenumber of FNCDs is 466 nm; the long-wavelength excitation can decrease cellular damage caused by the excitation light. The surfaces of FNCDs possess a positive charge, and thus can easily absorb into DNA and RNA, targeting the nucleoli and chromosomes of cells. The experimental cellular uptake of FNCDs indicated that the endocytosis of FNCDs involves pathways mainly including temperature-/energy-dependent transport, macropinocytosis, CME, CvME, and LrME. The CLSM images of FNCD-treated 4T1 cells demonstrate that the FNCDs can enter into the cell and stain the nucleolus and chromosome in different stages of the cell cycle. More importantly, FNCDs can be used as fluorescent probes to monitor the living cell cycle in real-time, and can completely display the whole cell division process. Due to their affordability and excellent optical stability, FNCDs will hopefully replace the commercial dyes, and become the new fluorescence dye for nucleolus and chromosome staining of living cells. This may open new ways of applying FNCDs in modern biology and cytology.

Hao Li: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Huibo Wang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Jiaqing Guo: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Shuai Ye: Data curation, Funding acquisition. Weilong Shi: Validation, Methodology. Xiao Peng: Funding acquisition, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Jun Song: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Junle Qu: Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.127891.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Two-dimensional fluorinated graphene: synthesis, structures, properties and applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Feng, P. Long, Y. Feng, Y. Li, Two-dimensional fluorinated graphene: synthesis, structures, properties and applications, Adv. Sci. 3 (2016) 1500413. https://doi.org/10.1002/advs.201500413.

C. Struzzi, M. Scardamaglia, N. Reckinger, J.-F. Colomer, H. Sezen, M. Amati, L. Gregoratti, R. Snyders, C. Bittencourt, Fluorination of suspended graphene, Nano Res. 10 (2017) 3151–3163. https://doi.org/10.1007/s12274-017-1532-4.

Technol. 228 (2019) 115770. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.seppur.2019.115770.

Choa, A noble gas sensor platform: linear dense assemblies of single-walled carbon nanotubes (LACNTs) in a multi-layered ceramic/metal electrode system (MLES), J. Mater. Chem. C. 6 (2018) 972–979. https://doi.org/10.1039/C7TC03576D.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Pliss, S. M. Levchenko, L. Liu, X. Peng, T. Y. Ohulchanskyy, I. Roy, A. N. Kuzmin, J. Qu, P. N. Prasad, Cycles of protein condensation and discharge in nuclear organelles studied by fluorescence lifetime imaging, Nat Commun. 10 (2019) 455. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-019-08354-3.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Song, Y. Sun, Y. Liu, X. Wang, M. Chen, F. Miao, W. Zhang, X. Yu, J. Jin, Low molecular weight fluorescent probes with good photostability for imaging RNA-rich nucleolus and RNA in cytoplasm in living cells, Biomaterials. 35 (2014) 2103–2112. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.biomaterials.2013.11.052.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots with intrinsic nucleolus-targeting capability for nucleolus imaging and enhanced cytosolic and nuclear drug delivery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.-W. Hua, Y.-W. Bao, F.-G. Wu, Fluorescent carbon quantum dots with intrinsic nucleolus-targeting capability for nucleolus imaging and enhanced cytosolic and nuclear drug delivery, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces. 10 (2018) 10664–10677. https://doi.org/10.1021/acsami.7b19549.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Hao Li was born in Jilin, China, in 1985. He received the B.S. and the M.S degree from Changchun Science and Engineering University, Changchun, China, in 2008 and 2011, and the Ph.D. degrees from Soochow University, Soochow, China, in 2016. Since 2018, he has been a Post-doctor at Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China. His research interests include preparation of carbon dots and their application in bioimaging and optoelectronic devices.

Huibo Wang was born in Jilin, China. She received B.S degree from North East Normal University, Changchun, China, in 2016. Since 2016, she has been a PHD candidate at Soochow University, Soochow, China.

Jia qing Guo was born in Shanxi, China, in 1992. He received the B.S. degree from Minnan normal University, Fujian, China, in 2015, and the M.S degrees from Minnan Normal University, Fujian, China, in 2018. Since 2018, he has been a PHD at Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China.

Shuai Ye was born in Hubei, China, in 1982. He received the B.S. degree from Xi’an University of Technology, Xi’an, China, in 2006, and the M.S. and Ph.D. degrees from the Beijing University of Technology, Beijing, China, in 2008 and 2012, respectively. Since 2018, he has been an associated professor at Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China. His research interests include nanoparticles systhesis, biophotonics, and bioimaging.

Weilong Shi was born in Jilin, in 1988. He received the B.S. degree from Changchun Normal University, Changchun, China, in 2010, the M.S. degree from Jilin Normal University, Siping, China, in 2015, and the Ph.D. degree from Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, China, in 2018. His research interests include preparation of carbon dots and their application.

Xiao Peng was born Jiangsu, China, in 1979. She received the B.S. and M.S degree from the Wuhan University, China in 2000 and 2003, and the Ph.D. degrees from the Hong Kong Polytechnic University, Hong Kong, China in 2007. From 2007 to 2010, She is a research assistant and assistant researcher in the Hong Kong Polytechnic University. Since 2014, she has been a lecturer at Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China. His research interest is bio-photonics.

Jun Song was born in Inner Mongolia, China, in August 1978. He received the B.Eng. degree in information engineering from Zhejiang University, Zhejiang, China, in 2001, and the Ph.D. degrees from Zhejiang University, Hangzhou, China, in 2006. Since 2017, he has been a professor with the Institute of Optoelectronics, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China. His research interests include nanophotonics and its applications in renewable energy, biomedicine, electronic information, and environmental protection.

Junle Qu was born in Shaanxi, China, in 1970. He received the B.S. degree from Xi’an Jiao Tong University, Xi’an, China, in 1992, and the Ph.D. degrees from the Xi’an Institute of Optics and FineMechanics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China, in 1998, respectively. Since 2003, he has been a Professor with the Institute of Optoelectronics, Shenzhen University, Shenzhen, China. His research interests include nanophotonics, biophotonics, microscopic imaging technique, and laser treatment technology.
